feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: Why does this fit the Digital Fairness Act
path: Why does this fit the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The idea of citizens to modify products they bought is pretty common in the offline world. People buy cars or home appliances fully expecting to be able to do with them as they please. Taking the example of a car, it is quite common for owners to get special tires specifically for snow, getting a new radio, adding dash cams, etc.

Often these modifications are bought from third party vendors and built into the car by auto shops often not associated with or licensed by the brand. Of the car directly. There's even a whole third-party market of shops buying of the shelf cars, modifying them and re-selling them to the customer.

When looking at digital goods today, which are mostly sold as services, this is simply not possible. While local modifications by the users themselves are in principle allowed, the resale of modified versions of the software is not. This means the idea that a company could buy a software license, make modifications to the software and resell the whole bundle, something that is every common in the physical world, is simply not possible in the digital world.

Additionally, even though local modifications are allowed in principle, most software nowadays is licensed under services to specifically prohibit that kind of modifications. While the companies can not directly disallow modifications to the software, they can license the software as part of a service, e.g. a subscription, where they can add terms that if they detect modifications to the software, they immediately revoke the access to the service and make the software unusable.

This disadvantages the user, stripping them of their right to use the products they paid for in the way they like to but also stifles innovation, as companies can heavily restrict what their software can be used for.

Additionally, the ability to at any time revoke access to the service and the associated software puts the users at an unfair disadvantage, where they have to comply with often even illegal requirements because losing access to the software could be worse (e.g. if they rely on this software for their income), than what they might gain from fighting this in court.

This is a proposal to target this on a legal level, to increase consumer protection, fairness and help to establish new markets for modifications and thereby increase innovation, competition and also sovereignty.

section_path: Why does this fit the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act tries to level the playing field for consumers and providers of digital services. The issues described above are the result of companies using their leverage through controlling access to their services to get an unfair advantage towards both consumers but also competition.

section_path: Why does this fit the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: This is a topic of digital fairness and consumer rights and would therefore fit well into the ambitions of the Digital Fairness Act as proposed by the Commission.

The idea would be to split digital goods between software products and services. Everything the user directly interfaces with on their device is a software product, while the functionality provided by any technical or organizational service components facilitate the service.
Then these two concepts need to be decoupled, where the service cannot require the use of specific software, but rather provide an interface which software can use.

For the use of software, the user then is granted the right to do modifications on the software, as long as it complies with the interface and requirements of the service, this newly modified software can then be used with the same service.

Additionally, software licenses should be made transferrable, including with modifications. This should allow companies to specialize on buying a specific product, e.g. Microsoft Office, add modifications to the product and resell this license with the modifications to an end user.

Lastly a big issue is companies restricting access to the services, e.g. if the market in a certain region is not profitable, or for political reasons, e.g. with the sanctions for Russia when the Ukraine war started.

As a lot of software and services is provided by American or Asian companies, this directly threatens European sovereignty, as those countries could restrict access of Europeans to those services for political reasons. For this purpose, a fallback should be also added, that if the official service is not available to use software, European companies can provide alternative services to be used with the existing software.
The proposed change will change the current landscape of software licenses a lot. In recent years more and more software is, instead of being sold as a product, only licensed as a service to users.

This results in monopolization of a few big tech companies, as they buy these services and integrate them into their larger portfolio. Because this software can't often be used as a standalone product anymore, those companies control the market and stifle competition and innovation.
Giving the users the right to break out of these monopolies will revitalize the software market and allow smaller software developers to contribute software or modifications to existing software.

Especially as there is already a big community of software developers, hackers and modders across Europe and access to resources for this kind of work is widely available in almost all European member states, especially also eastern European ones, this kind of market is well suited for the EU and could help revitalize the European economy in the digital market.
This should result in a more vital market, more competition and innovation.

Additionally, none of the suggestions will harm existing software contracts. Resellers of modifications are still required to buy the original software license, so the company selling those licenses will still get their share. Also, the existing services can still be licensed as subscriptions, users are only able to use them with different and augmented software, allowing for more competition.

Giving the users the freedom to get modifications for their software will improve the availability of software and features for different groups. This for example allows for developers to specialize on making existing software accessible to disabled people, or fit specific requirements, e.g. specific legal requirements in certain jurisdictions, and thereby make it more feasible for different markets.

It also will allow users to choose alternatives to software from the big tech solutions. For example, companies could take software like Microsoft Office and remove the reliance on Microsoft online services, to provide a more privacy friendly alternative, where the software does not communicate with American servers all the time.
Additionally, security researchers could identify vulnerabilities in software and provide fixes for those, not having to rely on the developers of the software to fix those issues.
This will provide users with the ability to get more privacy friendly, more secure, and better suited software for different user groups with different needs.

As described above, this also improves the digital sovereignty of the EU, as most big software companies are located in America or Asia, right now there is a big digital dependence of the EU on those countries.

By establishing such a legal framework for modification to software and also potentially alternatives to foreign services, Europeans could still enjoy all the benefits of using this well-established software from abroad, while not having to go into a full dependence on those foreign companies.

In case of sanctions or corporate overreach, European companies can jump in to provide the required modifications or services to the software and ensure that Europeans can still use their software as needed, if the foreign developers do not cooperate.

In the event another nation state tries to sanction the EU and withholding access to the services, the local market of modification developers can jump in to develop and provide the missing services, having European citizens not lose access to the tools and services required for their work.
